retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of tofacitinib. Describe the potential adverse effects of tofacitinib Review the appropriate monitoring for patients receiving tofacitinib Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance tofacitinib use in treating rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ulcerative colitis (UC), and polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA) and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib exerts its mechanism of action by inhibiting intracellular cytoplasmic nonreceptor tyrosine kinase JAK enzymes, which are involved in adaptive and innate immune reactions in the process of immune-mediated inflammatory diseases (IMIDs). [6] The Janus kinases are of four tyrosine kinase subtypes(JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, and TYK2). [6] Tofacitinib exhibits its inhibitory effects more selectively to the JAK1 and JAK3 enzymes, further restricting intracellular growth factor and cytokine-mediated signals to be transduced by the JAK-STAT pathway. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib is FDA approved for the treatment of moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ulcerative colitis (UC), and polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA). It is a second-generation selective Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitor targeting the JAK1 enzyme. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors pertinent to interprofessional team members in the management of patients with moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ulcerative colitis (UC), and polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA) that is unresponsive to first-line therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The intracellular Janus kinases' natural role is to phosphorylate the signal transducers and activators of transcription (STATs) enzymes which further influence gene expression and impact hematopoiesis and immune cell function. [6] The JAK-STAT signaling pathway plays a major role in the pathogenesis of autoimmune diseases, such as RA. Similar to other JAK inhibitors, tofacitinib blocks the phosphorylation and intracellular activation of signal transducers and activators of transcription, further diminishing their inflammatory effects. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Indications -- FDA Approved Use
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatoid arthritis Psoriatic arthritis Ulcerative colitis Polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis

retrieved_docs.title: Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs (DMARD) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Biologics, on the other hand, are very selective in their mechanism of action. The overarching functions of biologics include (1) interfering with cytokine function or production, (2) inhibiting the “second signal” required for T-cell activation, or (3) depleting B-cells or inhibiting factors that active B-cells. Tofacitinib is a small molecule inhibitor of JAK, a protein tyrosine kinase involved in mediating cytokine signaling. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Function -- Anti-inflammatory properties of Baricitinib
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib is a selective and reversible inhibitor of Janus Kinases (JAK) JAK1 and JAK2 originally approved for use in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Its use has been justified by proven dampening of the inflammatory response [5] . The JAK/STAT pathway has been identified as one of the subcellular pathways that mediate signal transduction of external stimuli to the nuclei of the cell, thereby mediating normal as well as aberrant immune responses. When cytokines attach to their receptors on the surface of the cell, a conformational change in the receptor leads to activation of the associated JAK complex. Once activated, the JAK complex autophosphorylates, creating a docking site for signaling molecules of the STAT family. The activated JAK complex also shows increased Janus Kinase activity leading to further phosphorylation of the STAT signaling pathway. The phosphorylated STAT molecules are then released from the receptor, translocate to the nucleus and lead to DNA sequence activation. This ultimately leads to target gene transcription [6] . The JAK signaling pathway family includes 4 proteins, the JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, and tyrosine kinase 2 (TYK2) [7] . Disruption of one or more of the JAK proteins is associated with an interruption in the signal transduction pathway and can lead to the beneficial effects of dampening a heightened immune response [8] . Cytokines implicated in causing the cytokine storm like the IFN-γ, IL-6, etc. have a JAK-mediated mechanism of action. IL6 is associated with JAK1, JAK2, and TYK2 [9] , whereas IFN-γ activity is mediated by JAK1/JAK2 [10] . Stebbing and colleagues validated the above-mentioned mechanism in their study. On evaluating the in-vitro pharmacology in relevant leucocytes as well as in-vivo pharmacokinetics of Baricitinib, they were able to demonstrate inhibition of IL-2, IL-6, IL-10, IFN-γ, and GM-CSF [11] . See Figure. Baricitinib Mechanism of Action

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Continuous communication between the interprofessional team, primarily the PCP and specialist, and their patients is imperative, and thorough education on treatment and disease should be provided. Updates on the latest guidelines on disease management using tofacitinib should be routinely reviewed. The patients should also be advised about the potential adverse effects while receiving treatment and the increased risk of severe infections. The patients should be advised to report any new-onset clinical manifestations. Period follow-ups visits with the PCP and specialist are essential to prevent complications, hospitalization, and mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib's indication as a treatment for polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA) is approved for subjects age two and older and available as an oral solution. The FDA approval was granted in September 2020 following a phase 3 clinical trial assessing its efficacy and safety in subjects aged 2 to 17 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib's indication as a treatment for ulcerative colitis (UC) was approved by the FDA following three randomized phase III placebo-controlled clinical trials (OCTAVE Sustain trial, OCTAVE Induction 1, OCTAVE Induction 2), which were conducted following a promising phase 2 trial. [4] The agent is the first medication in the JAK inhibitors class of drugs to be approved in the UC management. [5] Similar to RA and PA, tofacitinib's indication in UC is for adult patients with active moderate or severe disease who have not demonstrated an adequate response to TNF blockers or intolerance to therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Janus kinases are phosphotransferases, and receptor engagement by cytokines activates their enzymatic function. Janus kinase inhibitors represent a new strategy for the treatment of immune and inflammatory diseases. Tofacitinib is a targeted synthetic small molecule that is an oral inhibitor of Janus kinases (JAKs). It is (JAK) inhibitor FDA-approved in April 2012 and indicated for its use in the management of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ulcerative colitis (UC), polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA). [1] Tofacitinib's action on the nonreceptor tyrosine kinase JAK enzymes is more preferential to the JAK-1 and JAK-3 enzymes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: There are 4 JAK proteins—JAK 1, JAK 2, JAK 3, and TYK2— that form different pairs in various cell receptors, creating homodimers or heterodimers. These JAK dimers phosphorylate the signal transducers and activators of transcription (STAT) proteins and activate intracellular activity, including gene transcription of inflammatory mediators, ultimately triggering an autoimmune response. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib therapy in females of reproductive age may cause a possible risk to a developing fetus during pregnancy and may also impair infertility in women of reproductive potential. Females who are lactating and receiving treatment are advised not to breastfeed 18 to 36 hours following the last dosage.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib can cause hyperlipidemia. Dose-dependent increases in the levels of low-density lipoprotein (LDL), high-density lipoprotein (HDL), and total cholesterol have been reported in patients receiving tofacitinib. Long-term safety data showed higher rates of pulmonary embolism and death when using higher-dose tofacitinib (10 mg bid) in RA patients compared to those treated with a TNF inhibitor.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib's indication for active psoriatic arthritis received FDA approval following two phase III clinical trials, OPAL Broaden and OPAL Beyond. [3] The trials included subjects with psoriatic arthritis receiving tofacitinib treatment with a 5 mg dosage twice a day in sequence with MTX or other conventional synthetic DMARD therapy. Similar to RA, it is indicated for psoriatic arthritis in adult patients unresponsive or developed interlace to one or more disease-modifying rheumatoid drugs (DMARDs) with an active moderate to severe psoriatic disease course.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1298
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a pharmacologic class that suppresses immune activity and includes a variety of distinct agents. The class has child entities such as inhibitory interleukin agents, mycophenolate, and mykofenolát mofetil (mycophenolic acid). Specific drugs that are classified as immunosuppressants include basiliximab, daclizumab, elipticiny, everolimus, and the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil (hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu). In addition, the broader category of immunosuppressants also encompasses koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), and takrolimus. These relationships illustrate the hierarchical organization of immunosuppressive therapy, linking individual agents to the overall class and highlighting the diverse mechanisms—such as interleukin inhibition and calcineurin inhibition—used to achieve immunosuppression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids classified as potent (group III) comprise the agents amcinonide, flutikason, budesonide, fluocinolonacetonide and fluocinonide, each of which is a member of this high‑potency corticosteroid group. These drugs share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are commonly employed in dermatologic, respiratory and other inflammatory conditions where a potent corticosteroid effect is required. Their classification together reflects similar pharmacologic strength, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy for patients needing potent corticosteroid treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. By inhibiting BTK, these drugs are employed in the treatment of certain malignancies and immune‑mediated conditions, reflecting their role as both anticancer and immune‑modulating therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1729
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) represent a class of high‑potency corticosteroid agents, and amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid are all members of this group. These four compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, which are commonly employed for their strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in the treatment of dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. Recognizing that amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid belong to the same potent corticosteroid class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy and anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles across these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1748
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad pharmacologic class that includes a variety of agents used to attenuate immune system activity. Members of this class comprise inhibitory interleukin, mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, daklizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, hydrochloride of mykofenolát mofetilu, koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová, muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus. Each of these compounds functions to suppress immune responses and is employed in clinical settings such as organ transplantation, treatment of autoimmune disorders, and management of inflammatory conditions, highlighting the diverse therapeutic options available within the immunosuppressant category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1485
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad therapeutic class used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes agents that act through diverse mechanisms, ranging from inhibition of interleukin signaling to blockade of calcineurin or mTOR pathways and direct depletion of lymphocytes. Representative members of the immunosuppressant family are inhibitory interleukinu (agents that inhibit interleukin activity), mycophenolate, and mykofenolát mofetil (mycophenolate mofetil). Additional compounds classified as immunosuppressants are antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum), azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab (daclizumab), elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, and hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil). Specific immunosuppressive drugs also highlighted within this group are kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus (tacrolimus). By recognizing the wide array of immunosuppressants and their distinct pharmacologic actions, clinicians can tailor immunomodulatory regimens to achieve optimal graft survival, control autoimmune activity, and minimize adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1422
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants are a pharmacologic class that suppresses or modulates the immune system and are employed in organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions requiring immune inhibition. Within this class, specific subcategories include inhibitory interleukins, mycophenolate, and mycophenolate mofetil (mykofenolát mofetil). A wide range of agents are classified as immunosuppressants: antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum), basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab, elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, and hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride). Additional members of the immunosuppressant group are koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus. Together, these drugs share the common function of dampening immune activity, though they differ in mechanisms—ranging from inhibition of interleukin signaling to blockade of calcineurin pathways or depletion of lymphocyte populations—providing clinicians with multiple therapeutic options tailored to specific clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1722
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants are a class of immunologic factors that act to dampen or inhibit the activity of the immune system. Within this class, several agents are identified as direct “children,” including inhibitory interleukin agents, mycophenolate, and mycophenolate mofetil (mykofenolát mofetil). A broad range of specific drugs and biologic preparations are also classified as immunosuppressants: antilymphocyte serum, cyclosporine (cyklosporiny), daclizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, and the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil (hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu). Additional individual compounds that belong to the immunosuppressant group are mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), koformycin, muromonab‑CD3, paramethasone, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Collectively, these agents are employed to prevent graft rejection in organ transplantation, to treat autoimmune disorders, and to manage other conditions where suppression of immune activity is therapeutically desirable. Their shared classification under immunosuppressants underscores a common mechanism of reducing immune‑mediated damage while highlighting the diversity of chemical structures and clinical applications within the group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2224
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad group of immunologic agents that are classified under the parent category “imunologické faktory.” This class includes a variety of drugs and biologic preparations such as antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, cyklosporiny, everolimus, hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu, mycophenolate, mykofenát mofetil, kyselina mykofenolová, paramethason and takrolimus. Many of these agents act by inhibiting interleukin activity (e.g., “inhibitory interleukinu”) and suppressing lymphocyte proliferation, thereby providing immunosuppression for clinical purposes. Mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mykofenát mofetil (hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu) are related to the active compound kyselina mykofenolová, while cyklosporiny and takrolimus are calcineurin inhibitors that share a similar mechanism of action. Everolimus functions as an mTOR inhibitor, azaserin is an antimetabolite, and paramethason is a glucocorticoid with potent immunosuppressive effects. Antilymfocytární sérum provides passive immunosuppression through the administration of antibodies against lymphocytes. Collectively, these immunosuppressants are employed to prevent organ‑transplant rejection, treat autoimmune diseases, and manage other conditions where modulation of the immune response is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2739
retrieved_docs.content: Immunologické faktory constitute a broader category that includes immunosuppressants, a class of agents used to dampen immune activity. Immunosuppressants themselves comprise a diverse group of pharmacologic and biologic products, including antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, elipticiny, everolimus, as well as specific drugs such as koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid) and takrolimus. Within this hierarchy, inhibitory interleukinu is identified as a child concept of immunosuppressants, reflecting its role in specifically inhibiting interleukin‑mediated immune pathways. This organized relationship underscores how various immunosuppressive therapies are interconnected under the umbrella of immunological factors, facilitating a clearer understanding for clinicians when selecting agents for graft rejection prophylaxis, autoimmune disease management, and other conditions requiring immune modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2127
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a diverse group of agents that dampen immune activity and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. This class includes inhibitory interleukin compounds, the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil, antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab, the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, and the fungal toxin gliotoxin. Additional members are cyclophosphamide (koformycin), mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), the glucocorticoid paramethasone, and the calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus. These drugs act through various mechanisms—such as blocking interleukin signaling, inhibiting nucleotide synthesis, antagonizing the IL‑2 receptor, inhibiting mTOR signaling, or modulating glucocorticoid and calcineurin pathways—to achieve immunosuppression and improve patient outcomes in settings where controlling the immune response is critical.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) comprise a class of high‑potency glucocorticoid agents that are used primarily for their anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. The specific drugs amcinonide, flutikason, budesonid, and fluocinonid are all members of this potent group III corticosteroid category, sharing similar mechanisms of action and therapeutic indications while differing in their individual pharmacokinetic profiles and approved clinical uses. Recognizing that these four agents belong to the same high‑potency corticosteroid group helps clinicians select appropriate topical or systemic therapy, anticipate comparable efficacy, and monitor for class‑related adverse effects such as skin atrophy, hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis suppression, and systemic glucocorticoid exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2595
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad pharmacologic class that includes a variety of agents used to dampen immune activity. Within this class, interleukin‑inhibitory compounds are listed as child entities, alongside the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug form mycophenolate mofetil (mykofenolát mofetil). Additional immunosuppressive agents identified as members of this class are antilymphocyte serum (antilymfocytární sérum), the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab (daklizumab), the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, the fungal toxin gliotoxin, and the salt form hydrochloride of mycophenolate mofetil (hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu). The class also explicitly encompasses the drugs mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), cyclophosphamide (koformycin), and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Together, these entities illustrate the diverse mechanisms—ranging from cytokine blockade and lymphocyte depletion to inhibition of nucleotide synthesis and mTOR signaling—by which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation in transplantation, autoimmune disease, and other clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad class of immunologic agents that modulate the immune response. This class includes specific drugs such as inhibitory interleukinu, mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab, elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu, as well as related compounds koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus. The biosynthetic pathways for antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, and basiliximab are characterized and can be described in detail. Biosynthesis, a fundamental biological process, can be qualified or modulated by numerous substances, including the amino acid cystein, the plant alkaloids protoveratriny, natural quinoline alkaloids elipticiny, the fungal metabolite gliotoxin, simple trisaccharides, and immunological factors (imunologické faktory). Immunosuppressive agents such as cyklosporiny and daklizumab influence biosynthetic activity in immune cells, while inhibitory interleukinu suppresses biosynthesis by down‑regulating immune signaling. Koformycin serves as a probe of biosynthetic enzyme function, and muromonab‑CD3 modulates T‑cell activation, thereby affecting biosynthetic pathways. The anabolic steroid testosteronpropionát impacts protein‑synthetic biosynthetic routes. Collectively, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of immunosuppressive therapies, their diverse chemical forms, and their integration with biosynthetic processes, highlighting their relevance for research, diagnostics, and therapeutic manipulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 459
retrieved_docs.content: Fluokortolon and desoximetason are both classified as potent corticosteroids and are included in the category “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations.” As members of this group, they share the pharmacologic characteristics of strong glucocorticoid agents, providing powerful anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and often being incorporated into various combination drug formulations. Recognizing their membership in this potent corticosteroid class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic regimens and anticipating the potency‑related safety considerations associated with high‑strength glucocorticoid therapy.
